sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 7, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Starting level 1. The board appears to have a split background (yellow left, orange right) with a large black shape in the center and two blue squares near the bottom. Need to understand the structure and goal.
- Plan: First inspect the segmentation to understand objects, then probe actions to understand mechanics.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 6 of about 30 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see! Looking at the right square area:
- Row 39: OOOOOOOOOOBBBB - there are black cells (BBBB) at cols 44-47, which is to the right of the orange corridor
- The right square is at cols 39-43, and above it at rows 39-43, there are orange cells followed by black cells
Wait, let me look more carefully. The right square is at cols 39-43 (5 cols wide). Looking at row 39:
- Cols 34-43: OOOOOOOOOO (10 orange cells)
- Cols 44-47: BBBB (black cells)
So the right square can still move up since there are orange cells above it.
But for the left square at cols 19-23:
- Rows 29-38: BBBBBYYYYYYYYY - black cells at cols 14-18, yellow at cols 19-28
Wait, the left square is at cols 19-23. Looking at rows 29-38, cols 19-23 would be yellow cells. So the left square should be able to move up further!
But the left square didn't move on the last UP command. Let me re-examine... Actually, looking at the output:
- Row 39: BBBBBSSSSSYYYY
The left square is at cols 19-23, and there are yellow cells at cols 24-27. Above at rows 29-38, cols 19-23 are yellow cells. So the left square should be able to move up.
Wait, maybe the squares only move when there's a clear 5-row passage? Let me check if the passage above the left square is wide enough for the 5x5 square to move through.
Actually, I think I need to reconsider. The squares are 5x5 objects. When they move UP, they need 5 rows of non-black cells above them. Let me check rows 34-38 for the left square position:
- Row 34-38, cols 19-23: All yellow (YYYYY)
So there should be room for the square to move up. But it didn't move on the last UP.
Hmm, maybe the squares can only move when ALL squares can move? Or maybe there's a different mechanic. Let me try DOWN to see if the squares move back down.
Actually wait - let me re-read the changes. The last UP only changed 2 border cells, not the squares. But the previous UP moved the left square. Maybe the left square hit a "wall" at some point. Let me check what's at rows 34-38 more carefully.
Actually, looking at the maze structure from earlier:
- Rows 29-38: YYBBBBBYYYYYYYYYYYYYOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOBBB
So there's a black wall section "BBBBB" at cols 14-18 (relative to the full row). The left square is at cols 19-23, which is to the right of this wall. The yellow passage continues above.
Wait, I think the issue might be that the passage narrows somewhere. Let me check rows 14-18:
- YYBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBYYYOOBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBB
Here at rows 14-18, there's a narrow passage "YYYOO" at cols 29-33 (relative to the full row). The left square at cols 19-23 would be in the black wall area at those rows!
So the maze has:
- Wide passages at rows 29-43
- Narrow passages at rows 14-18
- Wide black area at rows 9-13
The left square is currently at rows 39-43. If it moves up, it would go to rows 34-38, then 29-33, then... at rows 14-18, the passage narrows to just "YYYOO" which is only 3 cells wide on the left side, not enough for the 5x5 square.
So the squares can only move through passages that are at least 5 cells wide. This is a maze where the squares need to find the right path.
Let me try LEFT and RIGHT to see if the squares can move horizontally.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>

# The left square is stuck because the passage narrows above.
# Let me try LEFT and RIGHT to see horizontal movement.
# First let me try LEFT

action(['LEFT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
